text.title: AI Prompt Engineering for Code
text.summary: Structured prompts with explicit context, task, requirements, and output format produce consistent, high-quality code from AI assistants. Iteration and specificity are more valuable than prompt length.

rule.statement: Every code generation prompt must include four components: Context (project type, tech stack, constraints), Task (specific action), Requirements (must-haves, edge cases), Output (expected format, file locations).
rule.rationale: AI tools without explicit context produce generic patterns. Explicit output format specification prevents the common failure of getting explanation prose when code was needed, or a partial file when a complete file was expected.

example.note: Structured prompt with all four components.
example.code: Context: Django REST API, Python 3.11, pytest, existing UserService class in src/services/user.py. Task: Generate pytest tests for UserService.create_user(). Requirements: cover validation error, duplicate email, successful creation, missing required field. Use pytest fixtures and parametrize where applicable. Follow AAA pattern. Output: Complete Python file at tests/test_user_service.py. No explanatory prose.
Write tests for UserService. Make them comprehensive.

rule.statement: Treat first AI output as a draft. Refine the prompt and regenerate rather than manually patching AI output unless the change is trivial.
rule.rationale: Manual patching of AI output accumulates technical debt — the prompt remains vague, producing the same low-quality starting point next time. Refining the prompt builds a reusable asset.

list.item: Draft: run the initial structured prompt.
Review: identify what is wrong or missing.
Refine: add missing constraints or context to the prompt.
Regenerate: produce a new complete output.
Final review: verify against requirements before committing.

rule.statement: For auth, payment, data access, and business logic: require a second human reviewer even after AI code passes automated review.
rule.rationale: AI-generated code may pass tests and static analysis but violate security constraints (e.g., logging sensitive fields, insufficient input validation). Automated tools cannot fully substitute human security review for high-risk code paths.
